user_goals_summary: This user writes reader-centric internal communications that follow a deliberate structural arc and end with concrete forward motion. They prioritize framing everything around the reader's reality rather than the sender's intent, maintain tight conciseness even when adding warmth, and treat the closing line as the most important sentence in any message.

rubric.name: Reader-Centric Framing
rubric.description: Every sentence about impact or stakes is framed around the reader's reality, not the sender's intent or comfort. 'Their plans shifted' rather than 'we want to make sure you have clarity.'

dimensions.label: Stakes and impact are described in terms of how the reader's situation changes, not what the sender is doing
Avoids 'I want to make sure you...' or 'we need to...' constructions when describing impact
The reader can immediately identify how the message affects their own work or plans
Acknowledges reader impact through concrete details rather than vague sympathy or reassurance

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Endings
rubric.description: The final line creates forward motion and gives the reader a concrete entry point — not reassurance, not a rally cry, not a status update. The reader finishes knowing their first move, already feeling in motion.

dimensions.label: Closing line references something specific the reader will do, not a mood or sentiment
Reader finishes feeling already in motion, not comforted or told things will be okay
No 'let me know if you have questions' or soft sign-offs after the key action
Reader can identify their immediate next move from the closing
Avoids punchy motivational slogans that substitute energy for direction

rubric.name: Deliberate Structural Arc
rubric.description: Information flows in a purposeful sequence where each beat has a distinct job: grounding acknowledgment before news, impact before action, next step before close. No beat is skipped or reordered.

dimensions.label: Opens with a grounding beat that acknowledges the situation before delivering the core message
Information flows in a clear sequence — context, then news, then impact, then action
Every sentence has a distinct job; no two sentences do the same work
Impact on the reader is stated before asking them to do anything

rubric.name: Conciseness
rubric.description: Every sentence earns its place. The message is scannable and stays tight even when adding warmth, acknowledgment, or context. Not a wall of text.

dimensions.label: Message length fits the communication medium and can be absorbed in one read
Each sentence carries a single, clear point
No sentence repeats what another sentence already communicated
Adding tone, acknowledgment, or context does not expand the message unnecessarily

rubric.name: Tone Calibration
rubric.description: Direct and professional without being cold. Warm through specificity, not through performative sentiment. Never tips into motivational language, over-apologizing, or hollow acknowledgment.

dimensions.label: Delivers information plainly without feeling impersonal or abrupt
Acknowledges effort or difficulty without sounding hollow, forced, or congratulatory
Avoids rah-rah phrasing that substitutes energy for substance
Addresses difficult news without excessive apology or hedging

rubric.name: Clear Ownership in Next Steps
rubric.description: Action items name a single owner and a specific timeline. The reader knows exactly who is doing what and by when — no diffused responsibility or vague collective action.

dimensions.label: Each action item names a specific person or team responsible
Deadlines or timeframes are attached to action items
The next step describes something concrete the reader will receive or do
Avoids 'we should all' or vague collective ownership of action items

coaching_notes: This user locks in elements progressively — once they say 'don't touch that,' treat it as fixed and focus revision energy elsewhere. They have a strong instinct for what doesn't work but may need multiple iterations to articulate what does; if they decline to give specifics twice, draft your best attempt rather than blocking. Their core litmus test for any sentence: does it serve the reader's reality, or the sender's comfort? If the latter, reframe immediately.

coaching_notes: This user has a finely tuned ear for the difference between sender-centric and reader-centric framing — even subtle constructions like 'I want to make sure you have clarity' get flagged. They will iterate extensively on a single line until it hits an exact emotional and functional target, especially closings. When they say 'this isn't quite there,' always ask for specifics before rewriting — they respond well to targeted questions and give precise, actionable feedback when prompted. Never sacrifice the structural arc (acknowledgment → news → stakes → action → forward motion) even when tightening for conciseness.

coaching_notes: This user has a strong instinct for structural order and will immediately flag when beats are missing or misordered — especially the opening acknowledgment, which they've flagged in every iteration. They consistently reject sender-intent framing and will keep pushing until impact is stated from the reader's perspective. Critically, they distinguish between specific-but-flat (logistics lists) and specific-with-weight (felt disruption) — specificity alone doesn't satisfy; the impact must land emotionally. When asked to clarify vague revision requests, they may repeat the request rather than elaborate — in those cases, make a best-effort attempt and name exactly what you changed so they can react to specifics.

user_goals_summary: This user writes concise, direct internal emails that deliver difficult or operational news. coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the opening acknowledgment beat — this user will flag its absence in every draft, regardless of email length or urgency. They treat abrupt openings as a tone failure, not just a structural one. Trade-off preference: will invest significant iteration time on a single closing line to get the exact quality of forward motion right; they'd rather delay sending than end on a soft or passive note. When asked for clarification on vague feedback, this user often prefers the assistant take a shot rather than ask repeated questions — after one clarifying attempt, produce a draft with your best interpretation.

Trade-off preference: will sacrifice warmth or cleverness for precision and clarity every time; a flat-but-accurate sentence beats a polished-but-vague one. Closing lines receive extraordinary scrutiny — this user distinguishes finely between reassurance, rally cries, status updates, and true forward motion; default to concrete reader action over mood or sentiment.

user_goals_summary: Writes concise, direct internal emails that respect the reader's context and reality. Every email follows a deliberate arc — acknowledge before informing, frame impact as felt consequence on the reader, assign clear single-owner next steps, and close with concrete forward motion. Stakes always land on what the reader is exposed to, never on the sender's intent or abstract process dependencies.

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Endings
rubric.description: The final line creates forward motion and gives the reader a concrete entry point — not reassurance, not a rally cry, not a status update. The reader finishes knowing their first move, feeling already in motion rather than passively waiting or simply informed.

dimensions.label: Closing line references something specific the reader will do or encounter, not a mood, metaphor, or sentiment
Reader finishes feeling directed toward action, not comforted or told things will be okay
No 'let me know if you have questions,' performative thanks, or soft sign-offs after the key action
Reader can identify their immediate next move from the closing
Avoids punchy motivational slogans or status updates that substitute energy or information for direction

rubric.name: Clear Ownership in Next Steps
rubric.description: Action items name a single owner and a specific timeline. The reader knows exactly who is doing what and by when — no diffused responsibility, no gesturing at implied internal structures. Secondary actions fold into the named owner's line rather than floating as separate group asks.

dimensions.label: Each action item names one specific person or team responsible — not a vague group
Deadlines or timeframes are attached to action items, concrete enough to anchor any downstream actions that depend on them
The next step describes something concrete the reader will receive or produce
Avoids collective ownership and does not gesture at implied owners inside the recipient's team — one person consolidates and delivers
Secondary actions that depend on a named owner are folded into that owner's line, not added as separate asks to the group that dilute ownership

rubric.name: Conciseness
rubric.description: Every sentence earns its place. The message is scannable and stays tight even when adding warmth, acknowledgment, or context. Not a wall of text.

dimensions.label: Message length fits the communication medium and can be absorbed in one read
Each sentence carries a single, clear point
No sentence repeats what another sentence already communicated
Adding tone, acknowledgment, or context does not expand the message unnecessarily

rubric.name: Tone Calibration
rubric.description: Direct and professional without being cold. Warm through specificity and respect for the reader's context, not through performative sentiment. Never tips into motivational language, over-apologizing, blame framing, or hedged softeners.

dimensions.label: Delivers information plainly without feeling impersonal or abrupt
Acknowledges effort or difficulty without sounding hollow, forced, or congratulatory
Avoids rah-rah phrasing that substitutes energy for substance
Addresses difficult news without excessive apology or hedging
Navigates problems or accountability without pointing fingers or assigning fault — frames constructively

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: opening acknowledgment before news, consequences framed on the reader (not the process), single-owner action items with deadlines, no trailing sentiment after the CTA. This user draws razor-sharp distinctions between similar-sounding phrasings — reassurance vs. direction, dependency map vs. felt consequence, status update vs. entry point — and will iterate until the nuance is exactly right. When they repeat 'could you write up a full draft,' produce a draft immediately rather than asking diagnostic questions; they steer better by reacting to concrete attempts than answering abstract prompts.
